6: YHWH’s provision for Elijah at the Wadi Cherith — wᵉhāʿōrᵉḇîm mᵉḇîʾîm lô leḥem ûbāśār babbōqer wᵉleḥem ûbāśār bāʿereḇ, ‘the ravens brought him bread and meat in the morning and bread and meat in the evening’ — is a type of divine provision through unlikely agents. Ravens are unclean birds (Leviticus 11:15); YHWH feeds his prophet through instruments the covenant would exclude from the sanctuary. The typological point: divine provision for the servant in extremity comes through unexpected channels, not through the established cultic system. Jesus in John 6:35 names himself as the fulfillment of the provision-from-heaven pattern: egō eimi ho artos tēs zōēs; ho erchomenos pros eme ou mē peinasē — ‘I am the bread of life; whoever comes to me shall not hunger.’ The ravens at Cherith are one in the sequence: manna in the wilderness, ravens at Cherith, flour/oil for the Zarephath widow, the feeding of the five thousand — all are types of the one true bread that descends from heaven and gives life to the world (John 6:33). The provision comes from above, to those in the wilderness, through agents the world does not recognize.
22: The raising of the widow of Zarephath’s son — wayyišmaʿ YHWH bᵉqôl ʾēliyyāhû wayyāšāḇ nefaš hayyeled ʿal qirbô wayyeḥî, ‘YHWH listened to the voice of Elijah, and the life of the child came into him again and he revived’ — is the OT’s first recorded individual resurrection. The sequence of acts — Elijah stretches himself over the child three times and cries to YHWH (vv21-22); the child lives — establishes the type-pattern: the prophet intercedes physically and personally with his whole body over the dead, YHWH hears, life returns. Luke 7:14-15 narrates Jesus’s raising of the widow of Nain’s son in deliberate structural echo: a widow, a dead son, a restoration, the crowd’s response that ‘a great prophet has arisen among us.’ Hebrews 11:35 in its honor roll of faith includes ‘women [who] received back their dead by resurrection’ — the Zarephath widow is the first. The sequence from Elijah’s raising to Elisha’s raising (2 Kgs 4), to Jesus’s multiple raisings (Nain, Jairus, Lazarus), to the general resurrection is the OT’s incremental witness that YHWH is the God who gives life to the dead — and Jesus is the one in whom that giving is no longer miraculous exception but constitutive identity: ‘I am the resurrection and the life’ (John 11:25).
1: A shadow: Elijah the Tishbite appears before Ahab with a drought announcement, swearing by YHWH of hosts. Jesus explicitly identifies John the Baptist as the Elijah who was to come (Matthew 11:14; 1 Kings 17:12–13; Mark 9:11–13; Luke 1:17), and Malachi 4:5 promises an Elijah-return before the great day of YHWH. Elijah himself is thus a type of the eschatological prophet who prepares the way — as John prepared for Christ's first coming, and as the prophetic witness of Rev 11 prefigures the final preparation for his return.
2: A revelation of God: the word of YHWH comes to Elijah immediately after his bold announcement — directing him to safety. YHWH does not send his prophet into danger and abandon him; he provides both the mission and the means of survival. Christ sends out his disciples with the same assurance: 'I am with you always, to the end of the age' (Matthew 28:20). The Elijah provision pattern is the OT ground of the NT promise.
3: A shadow: Elijah is commanded to hide by the brook Cherith east of the Jordan — withdrawal into concealment. Jesus also withdrew to desolate places to pray (Luke 5:16; Mark 1:35) and deliberately avoided premature disclosure (Mark 1:44; John 7:4–10). The hiddenness of the prophet in dangerous times anticipates the hiddenness of the Son of Man before his appointed hour (John 7:30: 'no one laid a hand on him, because his hour had not yet come').
4: A shadow: YHWH commanded the ravens (ʿōrᵉḇîm) to feed Elijah — unclean birds as divine provision agents. Jesus's teaching draws directly on this provision pattern: 'consider the ravens: they neither sow nor reap, they have neither storehouse nor barn, and yet God feeds them' (Luke 12:24). The ravens who fed Elijah model the providential care that Christ invites his disciples to trust — care that operates outside the normal categories of clean/unclean, expected/unexpected.
5: A theme: Elijah's immediate obedience — he went and did according to the word of YHWH. The pattern of prophetic obedience to divine direction anticipates Christ's obedience, the ground of salvation (Romans 5:19: 'by the one man's obedience the many will be made righteous'). The Elijah obedience narratives collectively are a shadow of the perfect obedience that will come.
7: A revelation of God: the brook dried up because there had been no rain in the land. The same divine word that announced the drought (1 Kings 17:1) is now experienced by the prophet — Elijah is not exempt from the physical consequences of the judgment he announced. Christ entered fully into the condition of fallen humanity: born under the law (Galatians 4:4), experiencing hunger (Matthew 4:2), thirst (John 19:28), and death — not as a victim of circumstance but as the one who bears the weight of the world's condition.
8: A revelation of God: the word of YHWH came to Elijah again at the moment of his need — the second commissioning follows the first provision's exhaustion. The pattern of divine direction that is responsive rather than exhaustive — YHWH reveals the next step when the current step is complete — is the theology of Proverbs 3:5–6 ('trust in YHWH with all your heart, and do not lean on your own understanding; in all your ways acknowledge him, and he will make straight your paths'). Christ as the Good Shepherd leads step by step (John 10:3–4).
9: A type: YHWH sends Elijah to Zarephath, a widow in Sidon — Phoenician territory, outside the covenant. Jesus explicitly invokes this episode in his programmatic Nazareth sermon (Luke 4:25–26): 'there were many widows in Israel in the days of Elijah... and Elijah was sent to none of them but only to Zarephath in Sidon, to a woman who was a widow.' This is a type of the salvation extending to the Gentiles: YHWH's provision bypasses the expected recipients (Israel) and reaches those outside the covenant. The Gentile mission of the church is prefigured here in Elijah's Sidonian commission.
10: A type: Elijah arrived at Zarephath, found the widow at the gate gathering sticks, and asked for water. The encounter at a well or gate between a prophet/divine representative and a foreign woman is a type-scene in Scripture (cf. Gen 24, Exod 2, John 4). The Samaritan woman at the well in John 4 is the NT fulfillment of this type: a foreigner, an outsider to the covenant, who receives living water from the one greater than Elijah (John 4:12–14).
11: A theme: Elijah's escalating request — first water, then bread — tests the widow's hospitality. The pattern of divine request exceeding easy compliance before miracle occurs anticipates the wedding at Cana (John 2:3–5: 'they have no wine' → 'fill the jars with water') and the feeding of the five thousand (Matthew 14:17: 'we have only five loaves' → 'bring them here to me'). The request that seems impossible becomes the occasion for divine provision.
12: A theme: the widow's oath 'as YHWH your God lives' — invoking Elijah's God while outside the covenant herself. The Phoenician widow's recognition of YHWH's existence anticipates the Gentile recognition of the God of Israel through Christ's ministry: the Syrophoenician woman (Matthew 15:22–28), the Roman centurion (Matthew 8:5–13), and ultimately the Gentile church. YHWH's faithfulness extends beyond covenant borders before Christ's coming; in Christ it is made universal (Ephesians 3:6).
13: A type: Elijah's command — make me a small cake first, before you make for yourself — is a demand for priority hospitality from a starving woman. This is both a test of faith and a type of the NT demand to 'seek first the kingdom of God' (Matthew 6:33). The widow's compliance models the radical trust that Christ calls his disciples to: giving the first and best to God even in conditions of apparent scarcity, trusting that provision will follow.
14: A direct type: the oracle that the flour will not run out and the oil will not fail until YHWH sends rain is the sign of miraculous ongoing provision. Jesus draws on this typology implicitly in John 6:35 ('I am the bread of life; whoever comes to me shall not hunger, and whoever believes in me shall never thirst'). The jar of flour and the jug of oil that are never exhausted are a type of the inexhaustible life that Christ gives — provision that does not run out because its source is YHWH himself.
15: A theme: the widow went and did according to Elijah's word — obedience preceding miracle. She and Elijah and her household ate for many days. The inclusion of 'her household' in the blessing of obedient faith anticipates the NT pattern of household salvation: 'believe in the Lord Jesus, and you will be saved, you and your household' (Acts 16:31). The Gentile widow's household, sustained by prophetic provision, is a type of the Gentile household brought into God's provision through Christ.
16: A direct type: the jar of flour was not exhausted and the jug of oil did not fail, according to the word of YHWH spoken through Elijah. The fulfillment formula marks this as a verified miracle of ongoing provision. The inexhaustible nature of the provision — sustained for months (1 Kings 17:15: 'many days,' 1 Kings 17:1: until the drought ends) — is a type of the inexhaustible nature of Christ's provision: the bread of life that feeds an unlimited number (John 6:11–13: twelve baskets of fragments remaining after five thousand are fed).
17: A shadow: after the miracle of provision, the widow's son fell ill and died — there was no breath (nᵉšāmāh) left in him. The sequence — miracle of provision, then death of the child — is the structure of divine testing that precedes a greater miracle. The pattern of 'first provided for, then tested through loss' appears in Abraham (provision of Isaac → demand to sacrifice Isaac) and in the disciples (miraculous feeding → storm on the lake). The greater miracle always exceeds the lesser provision.
18: A revelation of God: the widow interprets her son's death as divine punishment triggered by the prophet's proximity — 'you came to call my sin to remembrance.' The theological instinct — that death reveals guilt before God — is the background to Christ's answer when asked about the blind man in John 9:2–3 ('Rabbi, who sinned, this man or his parents?'). Christ corrects this simple equation while not denying the connection between sin and death: the blindness is 'so that the works of God might be displayed' — and so with the widow's son.
19: A type: Elijah took the child from the widow's arms, carried him to the upper room, and laid him on his own bed. The prophet's intimate identification with the dead child — placing him on his own bed in the prophet's own private space — prefigures the NT's language of Christ bearing human death in his own body (1 Peter 2:24: 'he himself bore our sins in his body on the tree'; 2 Corinthians 5:21: 'he made him to be sin who knew no sin'). The prophet's physical embrace of the dead is a type of the incarnation's embrace of human mortality.
20: A revelation of God: Elijah's prayer to YHWH is a bold protest — 'have you brought evil on this widow with whom I am staying?' The prophetic prayer that challenges divine action is a model of intimate covenant speech, not irreverent complaint. Jesus himself echoes this mode on the cross: 'my God, my God, why have you forsaken me?' (Matthew 27:46 = Psalms 22:1). The cry of dereliction is the ultimate expression of the prophetic prayer that dares to address divine action directly.
23: A type: Elijah took the child down from the upper room and gave him to his mother. The restoration of the dead son to his grieving mother is one of the three OT resurrection types that the NT gospels echo: the widow of Zarephath here (1 Kings 17:23), the Shunammite woman's son (2 Kings 4:36–37), and the widow of Nain's son (Luke 7:11–17 — the closest NT parallel, with the explicit formula 'he gave him to his mother,' Luke 7:15). Luke 7:15 quotes this verse's action almost verbatim, connecting Jesus's miracle to Elijah's as type to antitype.
24: A type: the Phoenician widow confessed, 'now I know that you are a man of God, and that the word of YHWH in your mouth is truth (ʾĕmeṯ).' This is the confession that a resurrection miracle produces: death reversed confirms the prophet's divine commission and the reliability of YHWH's word. The NT's supreme confession follows the supreme resurrection: 'my Lord and my God' (John 20:28, Thomas after the resurrection). The Zarephath widow's confession is the forerunner of the resurrection-generated faith that is the ground of Christian confession.
21: A direct type: Elijah stretched himself over the dead boy three times and cried, 'O YHWH my God, let this child's life return to him.' The threefold stretching (wayyitmōdad ʿal hayyelεḏ šālōš pᵉʿāmîm) — the prophet's body extended over the child's dead body — is the most vivid OT type of resurrection through bodily identification. The NT antitype is Christ's incarnation: 'the Word became flesh and dwelt among us' (John 1:14). God in Christ does not merely pray over humanity's death from a distance; he enters into it bodily and calls life back from within. The three-fold action anticipates the three days of Christ's death and resurrection — the pattern of death-embrace leading to life.
